title: Factors and Multiples
blurb: Factors, multiples, primes, GCF, and LCM — built from rectangles and skip-counting.

lessons.title: Factors & Factor Pairs
lessons.concreteComparison: You have 12 cookies to set out in equal rows. You could make $1 \times 12$, $2 \times 6$, or $3 \times 4$ — but not 5 rows, because 12 won't split into 5 equal groups. The numbers that **divide evenly** into 12 are its **factors**.
lessons.objective: Find the factors of a number as the whole numbers that divide it evenly, and list them in factor pairs.
concept: A **factor** of a number divides it **evenly**, with no remainder. $3$ is a factor of $12$ because $12 \div 3 = 4$.
Factors come in **pairs** that multiply to the number: $12 = 1 \times 12 = 2 \times 6 = 3 \times 4$.
Find them all by checking $1, 2, 3, \ldots$ in order: the factors of $12$ are $1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12$.
lessons.keyIdea: A factor divides a number with no remainder. Factors come in pairs that multiply to the number.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting that 1 and the number itself are always factors.
commonMistakes.correction: Every number has $1$ and itself as factors: for 12 that's $1$ and $12$.

commonMistakes.text: Listing a multiple instead of a factor.
commonMistakes.correction: A factor divides into the number ($3$ into $12$); a multiple is built from it ($24$ is a multiple of 12).

steps.title: Arranging into rectangles
steps.teach: Try to arrange all $12$ squares into a complete rectangle. Change the width: when it fits perfectly (no gaps), the width and height are a **factor pair** of 12. Widths that leave a short row are not factors.

steps.title: Factors divide evenly
beats: A **factor** divides a number with **no remainder**.
$12 \div 3 = 4$ exactly, so $3$ is a factor of $12$.
On the grid, factors are the widths that fill a complete rectangle.

steps.title: Factors come in pairs
beats: Each rectangle gives a **pair** of factors that multiply to the number.
$1$ and the number itself are always a pair.

steps.title: Finding factors
steps.intro: Check what divides evenly, and pair them up.

steps: A remainder means it doesn't divide evenly — not a factor.

steps.title: Listing all factors in order
beats: Start at $1$ and work up, checking each number.
Each time one divides evenly, write it **and** its partner.
Stop when the pair starts to repeat.

steps.title: All the factors
steps.intro: List each factor pair, then write them in order.

steps.title: Square numbers have an odd count
steps.body: Most numbers have an even number of factors because they pair up. But a **square** like $16$ has a pair that repeats ($4\times4$), so it counts once — giving an odd total: $1, 2, 4, 8, 16$ (five factors).

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **factor** divides a number with no remainder.
Factors come in **pairs** that multiply to the number: $3 \times 4 = 12$.
List them by checking $1, 2, 3, \ldots$ and pairing as you go.
